description: Focus on Polish literature, culture, language, linguistics, cinema, etc.; intensive study of an author or authors, a period, a literary or cultural development, linguistic phenomena, etc. Readings and lectures in English. May be repeated up to 3 times with a different topic for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: The Bold and the Restless: Polish Film from the 1950s to the Present
description: An exploration of the post-World War II history of Polish cinema, made famous worldwide by directors such as Wajda, Kieslowski, and Polanski. Topics of interest include the cinema of moral anxiety (1970s); absurd comedies depicting life under communism; adaptations of literary classics; and new topics and genres in contemporary Polish film.

title: The Structure of Polish (–3 cr.)
description: P: GER-G448, GER-G451, HISP-S326, HISP-S425, LING-L307, LING-L310, or LING-L431; or completion of the second semester of Polish; or completion of the fourth semester of another Slavic language (Czech; Russian; Bosnian/Croatian/Serbian; Ukrainian); or instructor approval.  Reviews the core topics in the synchronic linguistics of Polish: word structure (morphology), Polish speech sounds, phonological processes, and issues in syntax.  Explores how the linguistic phenomena are different or similar to those found in other, especially Slavic, languages

title: Fourth-Year Polish I
description: Refinement of active and passive language skills, with emphasis on vocabulary building and word usage.Â  Extensive readings, discussion, composition writing.Â  Individualized remedial drill in grammar and pronunciation.

title: Fourth-Year Polish II
description: Refinement of active and passive language skills, with emphasis on vocabulary building and word usage.Â  Extensive readings, discussion, composition writing.Â  Individualized remedial drill in grammar and pronunciation.

title: Summer Intensive Elementary Macedonian I
description: No previous knowledge of Macedonian required. Introduction to basic structure of contemporary Macedonian language and to its culture. Reading and discussion of basic texts. Credit given for only one of SLAV-Q 101, SLAV-Q 111, or SLAV-Q 311.

title: Summer Intensive Elementary Macedonian II
description: P: SLAV-Q 101, SLAV-Q 111, or SLAV-Q 311 with a grade of C or higher; or consent of department.  Continuation of SLAV-Q 111. Credit given for only one of SLAV-Q 102, SLAV-Q 122, or SLAV-Q 312.

title: Elementary Russian I
description: Introduction to contemporary Russian and aspects of Russian culture. Intensive drill and exercises in basic structure; development of vocabulary. Credit given for only one of SLAV-N 111, SLAV-R 101, or SLAV-W 301.

title: Elementary Russian II
description: P: SLAV-R 101 with a grade of C or higher; or appropriate placement exam score; or consent of department.  Introduction to contemporary Russian and aspects of Russian culture. Intensive drill and exercises in basic structure; development of vocabulary. Credit given for only one of SLAV-N 112, SLAV-N 122, SLAV-R 102, SLAV-W 302, or SLAV-W 351.

title: Russian Short Fiction
description: Masterpieces of Russian short fiction in a variety of literary modes, from the early nineteenth century to the present, with particular attention to Russian writers and works that have influenced the short story worldwide. Authors include Pushkin, Gogol, Turgenev, Chekhov, Babel, and Nabokov.

title: Russian for Heritage Speakers I
description: P: Consent of department.  Addresses specific needs of heritage speakers. Develops aspects of speaking, reading, writing, grammar, and cultural knowledge necessary to promote professional level proficiency, with special attention to vocabulary expansion. Cyrillic computer literacy and exposure to Russian culture through time. Students continue with SLAV-R 202 or higher.

title: Intermediate Russian I
description: P: SLAV-R 102 with a grade of C or higher; or appropriate placement exam score; or consent of department.  Continuation of work in structure and vocabulary acquisition through study of grammar, drills, and readings. Oral practice and written exercises. Credit given for only one of SLAV-R 201, SLAV-N 221, SLAV-N 231, SLAV-W 303, or SLAV-W 352.

title: Intermediate Russian II
description: P: SLAV-R 201 with a grade of C or higher; or appropriate placement exam score; or consent of department.  Continuation of work in structure and vocabulary acquisition through study of grammar, drills, and readings. Oral practice and written exercise. Credit given for only one of SLAV-N 232, SLAV-N 242, SLAV-R 202, SLAV-W 304 or SLAV-W 353.

title: Introduction to Russian Culture
description: Survey of development of Russian culture and thought from medieval Russia to the present, as seen primarily through literature and the arts. No knowledge of Russian is necessary.

title: Contemporary Russian Culture
description: Survey of Russian culture from the late Soviet period to the present. Concentration on three simultaneous dichotomies: anthropological versus artistic culture; nationalism and classic Russian themes versus Western and postmodern trends; and popular versus serious art, music, and literature. Knowledge of Russian not required.

title: Russian Folk Tales
description: Survey, analysis, and origins of traditional Russian folk tales based on thematic and structural classifications. Various approaches and theories are introduced in analyzing and interpreting folk tales, e.g., structural, formalist, thematic, and psychological. Pagan mythology, customs, and rituals are viewed as the possible origins of folktales. Influence of folk tales in music, arts, and cinema.

title: Pushkin to Dostoevsky
description: The golden age of the Russian novel; its social, cultural, and economic context; the flowering of art and music; the rise of the metropolis in association with poverty, alienation, quest for identity (both national and personal), as reflected in the romantic and realistic works of Pushkin, Gogol, Turgenev, and Dostoevsky. Knowledge of Russian not required.

title: Rus Lit: Tolstoy to Solzhenitsyn
description: 1880 to present, a period of profound political, social, and intellectual ferment: the Bolshevik Revolution, Civil War, collectivization, the Stalinist purges, World War II, the collapse of the Soviet Union, and the rise of a "new" Russia. Knowledge of Russian not required.

title: Advanced Intermediate Russian I
description: P: SLAV-R 202 with a grade of B or higher; or appropriate placement exam score; or consent of department.  Morphological, lexical, and syntactic analysis of a broad spectrum of textual materials with special emphasis on meaning. Development of oral and written fluency and comprehension. Remedial grammar and phonetics as required. Credit given for only one of SLAV-N 341, SLAV-N 351, SLAV-R 301, SLAV-W 305, or SLAV-W 354.

title: Advanced Intermediate Russian II
description: P: SLAV-R 301 with a grade of B or higher; or appropriate placement exam score; or consent of department.  Morphological, lexical, and syntactic analysis of a broad spectrum of textual materials with special emphasis on meaning. Development of oral and written fluency and comprehension. Remedial grammar and phonetics as required. Credit given for only one of SLAV-N 352, SLAV-N 362, SLAV-R 302, SLAV-W 306, or SLAV-W 355.

title: Advanced Intermediate Oral Russian I
description: P: SLAV-R 202 or consent of the department.  Designed primarily for those interested in developing oral fluency. Sections in advanced conversation, recitation, and oral comprehension, supplemented by lab and drill in corrective pronunciation, dictation, and reading. May be repeated for a maximum of 2 credit hours.

title: Advanced Intermediate Oral Russian II
description: P: SLAV-R 301 or SLAV-R 325; or consent of department.  Continuation and advanced treatment of topics covered in SLAV-R 325, as well as themes relating to current events. May be repeated for a maximum of 2 credit hours.

title: Nabokov
description: Provides an overview of Vladimir Nabokov's work in both Russian and English and contextualizes that work within the Russian diaspora. Explores the themes that make Nabokov a central figure of twentieth-century literature. Includes samples of literature by other Russian émigré authors. Knowledge of Russian not required.

title: Topics in Russian Literature, Culture, and Language
description: Focus on Russian literature, culture, language, linguistics, cinema, etc.; intensive study of an author or authors, a period, a literary or cultural development, linguistic phenomena, etc. Readings and lectures in English. May be repeated up to 3 times with a different topic for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: Russian and Soviet Film
description: Development of Russian cinematography from 1896 to the present. Characteristic features of Soviet films; the theory and practice of filmmaking in the former Soviet Union; the Soviet and Russian cinema in its relationship to Russian literature and in the larger context of European cinema art. Knowledge of Russian not required.

title: Russian Flagship Advanced Seminar
description: P: Admission into the IU Russian Flagship program; and consent of IU Russian Flagship administration. C: Enrollment in advanced Russian course.  Focuses on identifying students' academic goals, professional objectives, and personal achievements and translating this information into strong academic and professional materials, such as personal statements and CVs, to be used in high-stakes scholarship competitions, as well as advanced degree program and job applications.

title: Dostoevsky and His Demons
description: Examination of homicide, suicide, patricide, and redemption through a study of the psychological novels of Fyodor Mikhailovich Dostoevsky. Primary attention will be paid to narrative style, symbolism, imagery, point of view, structure, themes, allegory, and so on.

title: Special Credit in Slavic Languages (1–8 cr.)
description: Students who successfully complete advanced Russian institutes at other universities, or special technical language programs, or the CIEE programs at St. Petersburg or similar programs in other Slavic or East European countries, or who skip sequential courses by advanced placement, will be given corresponding undistributed credit as certified by the department. May be repeated for a maximum of 14 credit hours.

title: Advanced Russian I
description: P: SLAV-R 302 with a grade of B or higher; or appropriate placement exam score; or consent of department.  Refinement of active and passive language skills, with emphasis on vocabulary building and word usage. Extensive reading, discussion, composition writing. Individualized remedial drill in grammar and pronunciation aimed at preparing students to meet departmental language proficiency standards. Credit given for only one of SLAV-N 461, SLAV-N 471, SLAV-R 401, SLAV-W 307, or SLAV-W 356.

title: Advanced Russian II
description: P: SLAV-R 401 with a grade of B or higher; or appropriate placement exam score; or consent of department.  Refinement of active and passive language skills, with emphasis on vocabulary building and word usage. Extensive reading, discussion, composition writing. Individualized remedial drill in grammar and pronunciation aimed at preparing students to meet departmental language proficiency standards. Credit given for only one of SLAV-N 472, SLAV-R 402, or SLAV-W 357.

title: Russian Phonetics
description: P: SLAV-R 302 or consent of department.  Elements of articulatory and acoustical phonetics and their application to a comparative study of Russian and English sound systems. Methods in teaching pronunciation and intonation. Error analysis and correction of student's own pronunciation in lab and drill sections.

title: Structure of Russian
description: Systematic description and analysis of the structure of modern Russian phonology, morphology, and word formation. Comparison to elements of English grammar is included.

title: Readings in Russian Literature I
description: P: SLAV-R 302 or consent of department.  Reading, in the original, of important Russian literary works of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Discussion and analysis of the works.

title: Readings in Russian Literature II
description: P: SLAV-R 302 or consent of department.  Reading, in the original, of important Russian literary works of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Discussion and analysis of the works.

title: Readings in Russian Culture, History, and Society I
description: P: SLAV-R 302 or consent of department.  Extensive translation from the original of selected works on Russian history, government, music, folklore, geography, culture. Discussion of both linguistic problems and content.

title: Readings in Russian Culture, History, and Society II
description: P: SLAV-R 302 or consent of department.  Extensive translation from the original of selected works on Russian history, government, music, folklore, geography, culture. Discussion of both linguistic problems and content.

title: Fifth-Year Russian
description: P: SLAV-R 402 with a grade of B or higher; or consent of department.  This course continues developing students' advanced skills in Russian, while focusing on a particular topic related to a diverse range of social, cultural, or historical domains. Language work includes writing, syntactical and stylistic analysis, academic speech, reading and listening activities. May be repeated with different topics for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: Fifth-year Russian II
description: P: SLAV-R 402 with a grade of B or higher; or consent of instructor.  Covers a range of socio-cultural, historical, and political topics. Develops linguistic and cultural proficiency in preparation for academic study or research in Russian. May be repeated with a different topic for a maximum of 18 credit hours.

title: Advanced Oral Russian I
description: P: SLAV-R 302 or consent of the department.  Designed primarily for those interested in maintaining or developing oral fluency. Sections in advanced conversation, recitation and oral comprehension, dictation, and reading. May be repeated for a maximum of 2 credit hours.

title: Advanced Oral Russian II
description: P: SLAV-R 401 or SLAV-R 425; or consent of the department.  Continuation of SLAV-R 425. May be repeated for a maximum of 2 credit hours.

title: Russian Poetry
description: P: SLAV-R 302 or consent of department.  Studies Russian lyric verse and its development through Russian literary history. Analyzes the most prominent works in the genre, from the pre-Pushkin period to the present day, and develops interpretive strategies. Considers the unique place of lyric verse in the Russian cultural tradition, its interactions with other cultural forms in Russia, and its origins in and influence on other literary traditions. Readings in Russian; discussion in English.

title: Political Russian
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Students will develop advanced language skills with a focus on international relations, economics, trade, national security, and arms control. Students will be exposed to such authentic materials as newspaper articles and audio excerpts from news broadcasts. Course goal is to develop functional proficiency in all basic language skills: reading, listening, speaking. Grammar review is part of the course.

title: Business Russian
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Acquisition of vocabulary and construction characteristic of business Russian. Reading of background literature. Special attention will be given to study of culture in business and non-business situations.

title: Russian for Graduate Students I (3–4 cr.)
description: Mastery of basic grammar followed by vocabulary building. Active control of Russian structure needed for productive reading; emphasis on problems of translation. Open with consent of instructor to undergraduates about to complete the B.A. requirement in another foreign language.

title: Russian for Graduate Students II (3–4 cr.)
description: Mastery of basic grammar followed by vocabulary building. Active control of Russian structure needed for productive reading; emphasis on problems of translation. Open with consent of instructor to undergraduates about to complete the B.A. requirement in another foreign language.

title: Elementary Bosnian/Croatian/Serbian I
description: No previous knowledge of Bosnian, Croatian, or Serbian required. Introduction to all four language skills: listening, reading, speaking, and writing. Surveys the cultures of Bosnia and Herzegovina, Croatia, Montenegro, and Serbia. Credit given for only one of SLAV-S 101 or SLAV-S 111.

title: Elementary Bosnian/Croatian/Serbian II
description: P: SLAV-S 101 with a grade of C or higher; or consent of department.  Continuation of SLAV-S 101. Introduction to all four language skills: listening, reading, speaking, and writing. Surveys the cultures of Bosnia and Herzegovina, Croatia, Montenegro, and Serbia. Credit given for only one of SLAV-S 102 or SLAV-S 122.

title: Summer Intensive Elementary Bosnian/Croatian/Serbian I
description: Intensive summer equivalent of SLAV-S 101. Credit given for only one of SLAV-S 101, SLAV-S 111, or SLAV-S 311.

title: Summer Intensive Elementary Bosnian/Croatian/Serbian II
description: P: SLAV-S 101 with a grade of C or higher; or consent of department.  Intensive summer equivalent of SLAV-S 102. Credit given for only one of SLAV-S 102, SLAV-S 122, or SLAV-S 312.

title: Intermediate Bosnian/Croatian/Serbian I
description: P: SLAV-S 102 with a grade of C or higher; or consent of department.  Continuation of work in structure and vocabulary acquisition through written exercises, study of word formation, drills, reading and discussion of short stories.  Credit given for only one of SLAV-S 201, SLAV-S 211, or SLAV-S 313.

title: Intermediate Bosnian/Croatian/Serbian II
description: P: SLAV-S 201 with a grade of C or higher; or consent of department.  Continuation of SLAV-S 201: work in structure and vocabulary acquisition through study of grammar, drills, and readings. Oral practice and written exercise. Credit given for only one of SLAV-S 202, SLAV-S 222, or SLAV-S 314.

title: Summer Intensive Intermediate Croatian/Serbian I
description: P: SLAV-S 102 with a grade of C or higher; or consent of department.  Intensive summer equivalent of SLAV-S 201. Credit given for only one of SLAV-S 201, SLAV-S 211, or SLAV-S 313.

title: Summer Intensive Intermediate Croatian/Serbian II
description: P: SLAV-S 201 with a grade of C or higher; or consent of department.  Intensive summer equivalent of SLAV-S 202. Credit given for only one of SLAV-S 202, SLAV-S 222, or SLAV-S 314.

title: Introduction to Balkan and South Slavic Cultures
description: Survey of the cultures of Slovenia, Croatia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Serbia, Montenegro, Macedonia, Bulgaria, Albania, and Romania, concentrating on the modern period. Lectures and readings in English. May be repeated with different topics for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: Introduction to Croatia
description: Explores Croatia and Croatian culture, cultural history, and language, focusing on both the country's modern challenges and opportunities, and the historical roots of the current situation and how these affect modern Croatian arts, food, music, lifestyle, politics and much more.

title: Advanced Intermediate Bosnian/Croatian/Serbian I
description: P: SLAV-S 202 with a grade of B or higher; or consent of department.  Morphological, lexical, and syntactic analysis of a broad spectrum of textual materials with special emphasis on meaning. Development of oral and written fluency and comprehension. Remedial grammar and phonetics as required.

title: Advanced Intermediate Bosnian/Croatian/Serbian II
description: P: SLAV-S 301 with a grade of B or higher; or consent of department.  Reading of literary texts from a variety of periods and locations in the Bosnian-Croatian-Serbian speech area. Sequence of readings in original parallels syllabus of SLAV-S 363 and SLAV-S 364 in translation. Review of grammar, syntax, and expansion of lexicon as needed.

title: Special Topics in Slavic Studies (1–3 cr.)
description: Study and analysis of literary and cultural issues and problems in the Slavic studies area. Variable topics ranging from a study of a single novel or genre to selected themes of Slavic literature in their historical and cultural contexts. Topics will vary from semester to semester. May be repeated with different topics up to 9 times for a total of 15 credit hours

title: Literature and Culture of the Southern Slavs I: Literature and Nationalism in the Balkans
description: Survey of literary and intellectual history of the South Slavs in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, with a special focus on the foundational ideology of nations and nationalism in this period. Readings and discussions in English.

title: Socialist Modernism
description: Survey of literatures and cultures of former Yugoslavia during the 20th and 21st centuries. Exploration of the unique Yugoslav aesthetic of socialist modernism and directions Bosnian, Croatian, and Serbian literatures have taken in the post-modernist period.

title: Topics in South Slavic Literatures, Cultures, and Languages
description: Focus on South Slavic literatures, cultures, languages, linguistics, cinemas, etc.; intensive study of an author or authors, a period, a literary or cultural development, linguistic phenomena, etc. Readings and lectures in English. May be repeated up to 3 times with a different topic for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: South Slavic Film
description: Examines important trends, movements, and filmmakers in the history of South Slavic film. Considers South Slavic film in relation to developments in art, literature, culture, and politics in the region and within the larger context of global cinematic arts.

title: Fourth-Year Bosnian/Croatian/Serbian I
description: P: SLAV-S 302 with a grade of B or higher; or consent of instructor.  Focuses on advanced speaking, listening, reading, and writing. Incorporates major cultural aspects of Croatia, Serbia, Bosnia and Herzegovina throughout the instructional materials.

title: Fourth-Year Bosnian/Croatian/Serbian II
description: P: SLAV-S 401 with a grade of B or higher; or consent of instructor.  Fourth-year second semester of Bosnian-Croatian-Serbian, focusing on advanced speaking, listening, reading, and writing. Major cultural aspects of Croatia, Serbia, and Bosnia and Herzegovina are also incorporated throughout the instructional materials.

title: Career Readiness for Slavic I
description:
Introduces students to the department and the university's resources, building community among majors and minors. Discusses the value of Bachelor's degrees and experiences and provides a structured way to think about career preparedness and the role of students' academic plans.
 S/F grading.

title: Career Readiness for Slavic II
description:
Second of two courses designed to prepare SLAV majors, minors, and others for academic and career goals after undergraduate studies. Students will complete materials needed for a job search or applying for advanced-degree programs, as well as 
nurturing of mentoring contact with faculty and staff. S/F grading.

title: Topics in Slavic Literatures and Cultures
description: Selected topics from Slavic and East European literatures and cultures. May be repeated with different topics for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: Central and East European Immigration and Ethnic Identity in the U.S.
description: An area studies exploration of the ways in which people of Central and East European heritage express and adapt their ethnic identities in a United States context. With departmental approval, may be repeated with a different topic for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: Introduction to the Slavic Languages
description: An introductory course on the linguistic analysis of Slavic languages. With departmental approval, may be repeated with a different topic for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: The Vampire in European and American Culture
description: Explores the eastern European origins of the vampire and how the vampire in its look, nature, vulnerabilities, and threat has changed over the centuries, from premodern belief systems to European literature to cinema and games, to subcultures today.

title: Central & East European Immigration and Ethnic Identity in the U.S.
description: Examines ethnic identity and its many manifestations through relevant disciplinary lenses in the social sciences; explores the history and development of Central and East European ethnic communities in Indiana, the Midwest, and the rest of the US; and asks what it means to be "ethnic" in America.

title: Introduction to the Slavic Languages
description: Examines the evolution from Indo-European through Proto-Slavic to over 18 modern Slavic languages and their sound and writing systems. Students acquire foundational concepts in linguistic analysis which they use to analyze an array of language materials, drawing conclusions about language evolution and the forces which shape it.

title: Topics in Slavic and East European Literatures, Cultures, and Languages
description: Focus on Slavic and East European literatures, cultures, languages, linguistics, cinemas, etc.; intensive study of an author or authors, a period, a literary or cultural development, linguistic phenomena, etc. Readings and lectures in English. May be repeated up to 3 times with a different topic for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: Central European Cinema
description: Broad cultural overview of Central European cinema, highlighting major developments of cinema in Poland, Hungary, Bulgaria, and the former Republics of Czechoslovakia and Yugoslavia in the post-Stalin era. Special attention will be given to the individual style and aesthetics of several major film directors.

title: The Structure of Slavic Languages
description: P: GER-G 448, GER-G 451, HISP-S 326, HISP-S 425, LING-L307, LING-L 310, or LING-L 431; or consent of the department..  The course covers topics in Slavic synchronic linguistics. Starting with peculiarities of word structure (morphology), over the articulation of sounds of speech (phonetics), and phonological effects, and ending with issues in syntax, we will show how the related languages select different strategies to deal with problematic linguistic structures.

title: Senior Honors Thesis (1–6 cr.)
description:
P: Approval of Director of Undergraduate Studies or the department chair.  Students write a departmental honors thesis (or similarly advanced, ambitious work to fulfill departmental honors, such as a substantial translation into English from a departmental language) under the guidance of a departmental faculty member.
 May be repeated up to 2 times for a maximum of 6 credit hours

title: Elementary Ukrainian I
description: No previous knowledge of Ukrainian required. First semester designed to provide active command of phonology and basic grammatical patterns of Ukrainian. Credit given for only one of SLAV-U 101, SLAV-U 111, or SLAV-U 311.

title: Elementary Ukrainian II
description: P: SLAV-U 101 with a grade of C or higher; or consent of department.  Continuation of SLAV-U 101, designed to provide active command of phonology and basic grammatical patterns. Credit given for only one of SLAV-U 102, SLAV-U 122, SLAV-U 312, or SLAV-Y 182.

title: Summer Intensive Elementary Ukrainian I
description: No previous knowledge of Ukrainian required. Intensive summer equivalent of SLAV-U 101, designed to provide active command of phonology and basic grammatical patterns of Ukrainian. Credit given for only one of SLAV-U 101, SLAV-U 111, or SLAV-U 311

title: Summer Intensive Elementary Ukrainian II
description: P: SLAV-U 101; or consent of department.  Intensive summer equivalent of SLAV-U 102, designed to provide active command of phonology and basic grammatical patterns. Credit given for only one of SLAV-U 102, SLAV-U 122, SLAV-U 182, or SLAV-U 312.

title: Ukrainian for Slavic Speakers I
description: P: One of SLAV-R 302, SLAV-C 302, SLAV-P 302, SLAV-S 302; or permission of the instructor.  Accelerated Ukrainian language course for students who have previous experience with Russian or another Slavic language. Develops students' proficiency in all language skills (speaking, reading, writing, and listening) and acquaints students with Ukrainian culture.

title: Intermediate Ukrainian I
description: P: SLAV-U 102 with a grade of C or higher; or consent of department.  Continuation of work in structure and vocabulary acquisition through study of grammar, drills, and readings. Oral practice and written exercises. Credit given for only one of SLAV-U 201, SLAV-U 211, or SLAV-U 282.

title: Intermediate Ukrainian II
description: P: SLAV-U 201 with a grade of C or higher; or consent of department.  Continuation of work in structure and vocabulary acquisition through study of grammar, drills, and readings. Oral practice and written exercises. Credit given for only one of SLAV-U 202, SLAV-U 222, or SLAV-U 282.

title: Summer Intensive Intermediate Ukrainian I
description: P: SLAV-U 102, SLAV-U 122, or SLAV-U 312; or consent of department.  Continuation of work in structure and vocabulary acquisition through grammar study, drills, and readings. Oral practice and written exercises.

title: Summer Intensive Intermediate Ukrainian II
description: P: SLAV-U 211; or consent of department.  Continuation of work in structure and vocabulary acquisition through grammar study, drills, and readings. Oral practice and written exercises.

title: Introduction to Ukrainian Culture
description: Survey of Ukrainian culture from Kyivan Rus to the present, including such diverse facets of Ukrainian culture as folklore, language, art, literature, and modern pop-culture. Readings are supplemented by documentaries, cartoons, and music. Knowledge of Ukrainian not required.

title: Ukrainian for Slavic Speakers II
description: P: SLAV-R 302; or SLAV-C 302; or SLAV-P 302; or SLAV-S 302; or SLAV-U 199; or permission of the instructor.  An accelerated Ukrainian language course for students who have previous experience with Russian or another Slavic language. Develops students' proficiency at the Intermediate level in all language skills (speaking, reading, writing, and listening) and further acquaints students with Ukrainian culture.

title: Advanced Intermediate Ukrainian I
description: P: A grade of "B" or higher in SLAV-U 202 or SLAV-U 299; or, permission of the department.  Develops students' ACTFL Intermediate Mid-to-High skills in speaking, writing, reading, and listening about a broad range of topics related to contemporary Ukrainian society and culture.

title: Advanced Intermediate Ukrainian II
description:
P: A grade of "B" or higher in SLAV-U 301; or, permission of the department
.  Develops student's ACTFL Intermediate-High skills in speaking, writing, reading, and listening about a broad range of topics related to contemporary Ukrainian society and culture.

title: Advanced Ukrainian I
description: P: SLAV-U 222 or SLAV-U 282 with a grade of B or higher; or consent of instructor.  Develops advanced skills in speaking, writing, reading, and listening about a broad range of topics related to contemporary Ukrainian society and culture. Emphasis is on fostering cultural awareness, a higher level of listening and reading comprehension, and more sophisticated self-expression in both writing and speaking.

title: Advanced Ukrainian II
description: P: SLAV-U 311 with a grade of B or higher; or consent of instructor.  Develops advanced skills in speaking, writing, reading, and listening about a broad range of topics related to contemporary Ukrainian society and culture. Emphasis is on fostering cultural awareness, a higher level of listening and reading comprehension, and more sophisticated self-expression in both writing and speaking.

title: Topics in Ukrainian Literature, Culture, and Language
description: Focus on Ukrainian literature, culture, language, linguistics, cinema, etc.; intensive study of an author or authors, a period, a literary or cultural development, linguistic phenomena, etc. Readings and lectures in English. May be repeated with a different topic for a maximum of 3 enrollments.

title: Ukrainian Cinema
description: Surveys Ukrainian cinema from its beginnings in the late 1890s to the present. Special attention is paid to the Soviet Thaw period of the 1960s, and Ukrainian cinema after independence in 1991 and subsequent Russian invasions.

title: Readings in Romanian (1–3 cr.)
description: P: SLAV-M 202 or consent of instructor.  Continuation of work in structure and vocabulary acquisition through grammar study, drills and readings, oral practice, and written exercises. May be repeated for a maximum of 12 credit hours in SLAV-M 350 and SLAV-X 390.

title: Undergraduate Teaching Internship in Slavic (1–3 cr.)
description:
P: Consent of department.  Supervised teaching experience in an undergraduate Slavic and East European Languages and Cultures course. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.
 S/F grading.

title: Internship in Slavic (1–3 cr.)
description: P: Consent of department.  Supervised work experience in a possible range of fields, such as research and publishing, as well as approved internships abroad.  Credit hours are tied to the number of internship hours worked.   S/F grading

title: Supervised Individual Reading (1–3 cr.)
description: P: Consent of the department.  Reading, in the original, of materials in field of Slavic studies of particular interest to student. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours in SLAV-S 498 and SLAV-X 490.

title: Independent Study in Slavic (1–3 cr.)
description: P: Consent of department.  For students conducting advanced independent study under guidance of faculty supervisor.  Study plan must be approved by supervisor; director of undergraduate studies may be asked to approve course for departmental major or minor. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: American Sign Language Level One
description: Introductory sign language course for students with no previous experience with sign language. Builds a good basic vocabulary of signs, teaches fingerspelling, introduces basic aspects of the grammar and the proper use of facial expression in sign language conversation. Includes exposure to deaf culture. Credit given for only one of SLHS-A 100 or SPHS-A 100.

title: American Sign Language Level Two
description: P: SLHS-A 100 or SPHS-A 100; or consent of instructor.  Continues building receptive and expressive abilities developed in SLHS-A 100. Emphasizes the use of signing space, facial grammar, body postures, fluent fingerspelling, and continued vocabulary development. Introduces more complex grammatical structures. Includes further exposure to deaf culture. Credit given for only one of SLHS-A 150 or SPHS-A 150.

title: American Sign Language Level Three
description: P: SLHS-A 150 or SPHS-A 150; or consent of instructor.  Emphasizes the development of conversational ability. Examines complex grammatical structures, emphasizing the ability to use these structures in conversation. Covers characteristics of the Deaf population and their cultural values. Credit given for only one of SLHS-A 200 or SPHS-A 200.

title: American Sign Language Level Four
description: P: SLHS-A 200 or SPHS-A 200; or consent of instructor.  Provides further study of ASL grammar, syntax, and cultural features. Develops competency and fluency in conversational skills. Utilizes vocabulary, grammatical principles, and various discourse features in narratives and presentations in ASL. Credit given for only one of SLHS-A 250 or SPHS-A 250.

title: Deaf Culture
description: Provides an overview of American Deaf culture, including cultural identity, core values, group norms, traditions, communication, language, and significant contributions made by Deaf persons. Views and perspectives of and from the Deaf community will be explored along with implications of cultural differences when the Deaf and hearing worlds overlap.  Credit given for only one of SLHS-A 400 or SPHS-A 400.

title: Human Communication and Its Disorders
description: Introduces the scientific foundation of human communication. Mechanisms underlying speaking and listening provide a framework for exploring core topics in speech, language, and hearing sciences.  Emphasis given to solving scientific and clinical problems, including how to develop and test hypotheses, analyze data, and investigate how disease and injury affect communication systems.

title: Hearing Science: An Overview
description: Introduction to hearing science and how it is applied toward the evaluation and treatment of clinical problems. Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 106 or SPHS-S 106.

title: Speech and Language Sciences: An Overview
description: Introduction to the development and use of speech and language across the lifespan and to the scientific foundations of typical spoken communication. Surveys the application of speech and language sciences to the understanding of some common disorders of speech and language.	 Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 108 or SPHS-S 108.

title: Survey of Communication Disorders
description: Introduction to behavioral and social aspects of communication disorders. Includes a broad overview of human communication with emphasis on development, adult functions and cultural differences, in addition to disorders. Also examines general approaches to rehabilitation of the communicatively handicapped, and current controversies. Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 110 or SPHS-S 110.

title: Introduction to Phonetics for Speech, Language and Hearing Sciences
description: Provides an introduction to the sounds of human language and the skills needed to describe speech sounds in the articulatory and acoustic domains and transcribe speech using the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA), including both broad and narrow transcription. Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 111 or SPHS-S 111.

title: Speech Anatomy and Physiology
description: Covers anatomy and physiology of the speech mechanism; contemporary views of speech physiology; subsystems of the speech mechanism - respiratory, laryngeal, and supraglottal - integrated with a model of speech production. Includes laboratory experiences. Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 201 and SPHS-S 201.

title: Topics in Speech, Language, and Hearing Sciences
description: Studies in special topics relating to language, speech, or hearing not ordinarily covered by SLHS program courses. Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 209 or SPHS-S 209

title: Honors Seminar
description: An undergraduate seminar for Hutton Honors College students. Focuses on the sciences of speech, language, and hearing within the context of human communication. May include such topics as evolution, bioacoustics, history, disorders, and more. Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 215 or SPHS-S 215.

title: Human Hearing and Communication
description: Examines human hearing and communication, including the physics of sound, auditory anatomy and physiology, and auditory perception; diagnostic audiology, including hearing assessment and screening; rehabilitative audiology, including an overview of hearing aids, cochlear implants, and educational issues for children with hearing loss. Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 275 or SPHS-S 275.

title: Acoustics for Speech, Language, and Hearing Sciences
description: P: SLHS-S 275 or SPHS-S 275.  Basic acoustics with the emphasis being on topics applicable to the speech and hearing sciences. Acoustics of the speech and hearing mechanisms. Instrumentation for sound production and recording. Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 302 or SPHS-S 302.

title: Cognitive and Communicative Aspects of Aging
description: P: SLHS-S 104, SLHS-S 108, or SPHS-S 108; and SLHS-S 201, SPHS-S 201, or ANAT-A 215.  Review of cognitive and communicative changes associated with normal aging as well as with diseases and conditions that are prevalent in the aging population. Includes discussion of methodological issues in research on aging as well as principles for maximizing communication with the elderly population. Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 307 or SPHS-S 307.

title: Introduction to Research in Speech, Language, and Hearing Sciences
description: P: PSY-K 300 or STAT-S 303.  Introduction to research methodology in speech and hearing science, both basic and applied. Focuses on basic designs, analyses, and how research informs clinical practice. Course includes discussion of ethics, evaluation of research, and scientific writing. Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 311 or SPHS-S 311.

title: Careers in Speech, Language and Hearing Sciences
description: P: Sophomore standing or above and SLHS-S 104.  Provides students with tools and opportunities to establish professional networks and develop application materials for graduate school tailored for a future career in speech, language and hearing sciences. S/F grading.

title: Childhood Language
description: P: At least sophomore standing.  Develops knowledge of how language is acquired by young children, examines data on what young infants and young children know about language at different ages, and considers the kinds of theories that may explain this data. Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 333 or SPHS-S 333.

title: Speech Science
description: P: At least sophomore standing; and SLHS-S 104, SLHS-S 108, or SPHS-S 108; and SLHS-S 201 or SPHS-S 201.  Provides an overview of speech production, including acoustics and physiology, as well as speech perception. The focus is on non-disordered speech in adults with some coverage of development as warranted. Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 350 or SPHS-S 350.

title: Hearing Science
description:
P: At least sophomore standing; and SLHS-S 104, SLHS-S 106, or SPHS-S 106.  Provides an in-depth study of hearing in the normal auditory system. Topics include anatomy and physiology of the ear, anatomy and physiology of the auditory brain, and auditory perception and psychophysics.

			 Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 375 or SPHS-S 375.

title: Honors Seminar in Speech, Language and Hearing Sciences
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Seminar in which students begin to think critically about conducting research in Speech, Language and Hearing Sciences. Focuses on the fundamentals of research design as well as the development of research-related skills, such as reading and understanding research and writing a scientific report. Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 398 or SPHS-S 398.

title: Senior Honors I: Reading and Research
description:
P: Senior standing; and approval of the departmental honors committee.  Continuation of work begun in SLHS-S 398. Focuses on the development of students' individual research projects, with a particular emphasis on research methods and data collection. Group meetings provide for review of general topics related to research, ethics, scholarly writing, and other topics of interest to individual students.
 Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 399 or SPHS-S 399.

title: Speech-Language Pathology Assistant Seminar I
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Provides the foundation necessary for working as support personnel to assist speech-language pathologists in public school settings. First in a series of two courses required for licensing as an Speech-Language Pathology Assistant. Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 417 or SPHS-S 417.

title: Speech-Language Pathology Assistant Seminar II
description: P: SLHS-S 417 or SPHS-S 417.  Extends the depth and breadth of foundational concepts covered in SLHS-S 417 necessary to provide speech and language intervention services to children in the public schools. Credit given for only one of SLHS-S 419 or SPHS-S 419.

title: Topical Seminar in Speech, Language and Hearing Sciences
description: P: At least junior standing.  In-depth study of specific topics in speech science, language science, and hearing science. Topics vary by semester. May be repeated with different topics for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: Topical Seminar in Speech Science
description: P: At least junior standing; and SLHS-S 350 or SPHS-S 350.  In-depth study of specific topics in speech science. Topics vary by semester. May be repeated with a different topic for a maximum of 9 credit hours in SLHS-S 425 and SPHS-S 425.

title: Topical Seminar in Language Sciences
description: P: At least junior standing; and SLHS-S 333 or SPHS-S 333.  In-depth study of specific topics in language science. Topics vary by semester. May be repeated with different topics for a maximum of 9 credit hours in SLHS-S 427 or SPHS-S 427.

title: Topical Seminar in Hearing Science
description: P: At least junior standing; and SLHS-S 375 or SPHS-S 375.  In-depth study of specific topics in hearing science. Topics vary by semester. May be repeated with different topics for a maximum of 9 credit hours in SLHS-S 429 and SPHS-S 429.

title: Assessment and Treatment of Hearing Loss
description: P: At least junior standing; and SLHS-S 375 or SPHS-S 375.  Covers basic acoustics, anatomy and physiology of the auditory system with a focus on the applied principles of audiologic evaluation, management, and (re)habilitation options for adult and pediatric populations. It is geared toward upper-level undergraduate students majoring in speech, language, and hearing sciences.

title: Senior Honors II: Reading and Research
description: P: Senior standing and approval of departmental honors committee.  Continuation of work begun in SLHS-S 399. Focuses on the development of students' individual research projects, with a particular emphasis on data analysis and interpretation of findings.

title: Undergraduate Teaching Internship in Speech, Language and Hearing Sciences (1–2 cr.)
description: P: Junior or senior standing, with priority given to Speech, Language and Hearing Sciences majors with overall GPA of 3.500; and consent of instructor.  Student interns assist in classrooms and participate in weekly seminars that include discussion of both theoretical and practical aspects of the teaching mission at Indiana University. May be repeated for a maximum of 4 credit hours in SLHS-X-471 and SPHS-X 471. S/F grading.

title: Speech-Language Pathology Practicum and Seminar (1–3 cr.)
description: P: Junior or senior standing; SLHS-S 104; and department consent required.  Beginning practicum experience in speech-language pathology, with seminar. May be repeated once for credit for a maximum of 6 credit hours in SLHS-X 476 and SPHS-X 476.

title: Audiology Practicum and Seminar (1–3 cr.)
description: P: Junior or senior standing; SLHS-S104; and department consent required..  Beginning practicum experience in audiology, with seminar. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: Readings in Speech, Language and Hearing Sciences (1–3 cr.)
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Individual study in an area of speech, language, and hearing sciences under the guidance of faculty member. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours in SLHS-X 490 and SPHS-X 490.

title: Research in Speech, Language and Hearing Sciences (0–3 cr.)
description:
P: Consent of supervising instructor and chair.  Supervised research in an area of speech, language and hearing sciences. A formal research paper or project is required. May be repeated up to 6 times for a maximum of 6 credit hours.
 S/F grading.

title: Pathways: Introduction to Art, Design and Merchandising
description: Explores the fields of art, design, and merchandising within the contemporary landscape of creating and making. Identifies where these disciplines have mutually reinforcing values and opportunities for interdisciplinary study. Provides a common experience for art, design and merchandising students.

title: Creative Core: Color
description: Explores color's fundamental principles and formal elements; its contextual meanings and sociological connotations; and its significance within the fields of art, design and merchandising. Cultivates visual sensitivity, develops aesthetic knowledge and the production of creative work through studio practices. Credit given for only one of SOAD-A 101 or FINA-F 102.

title: Creative Core: Drawing
description: Development of visual awareness and coordination of perceptual and manual skills; seeing, representing, and inventing on an experimental, exploratory level in two dimensions. Includes placement, scale, volume, light, formal articulation, and investigation of graphic tools and media. Credit given for only one of FINA-F 100 or SOAD-A 102.

title: Creative Core: 3D Design
description: Volume, space, material, and physical force studies provide the basis for exploration of three-dimensional form; includes carving, construction, modeling, and casting using wood, plaster, Styrofoam, clay, etc. Credit given for only one of FINA-F 101 or SOAD-A 103.

title: Creative Core: Design Thinking
description: Explores Design Thinking as an alternative approach to traditional problem-solving. Focuses on user-centric and prototype-driven design, starting without any pre-conceived problems or solutions about the user(s) or context, freeing the mind to discover alternate paths to reach a goal. Students take part in the iterative design thinking process, as prototypes are designed and tested, and often redesigned.

title: Forum of Exchange I
description: Interdisciplinary experiences serve as the foundation for discussion and written/verbal responses. Students select from a range of activities including lectures, cross-critiques, site visits, interviews, class visits, museum exhibitions, and performances to observe, question and compare art, design and merchandising disciplines.  May be repeated for a maximum of 4 credit hours. S/F grading.

title: Studio Art BA Capstone (1–3 cr.)
description: P: Studio Art BA major; and senior standing or consent of instructor.  Supports students in developing a cohesive body of work through critique, project development, and a final public exhibition. Emphasis is placed on professional practices including proposal writing, promotion, and portfolio development, while fostering meaningful academic and professional relationships and crafting compelling, field-appropriate personal narratives relative to art and design.  May be repeated for credit up to 2 times for a maximum of 3 credit hours.

title: Forum of Exchange II
description: Interdisciplinary experiences in art, design and merchandising serve as the foundation for discussion and written/verbal responses. Students select from a range of activities including lectures, cross-critiques, site visits, interviews, class visits and selected exhibitions.  May be repeated for a maximum of 4 credit hours.  S/F grading.

title: Digital 3D Modeling for Comprehensive Design (3–3 cr.)
description: Focuses on graphic communication and 3D modeling abilities. Provides foundation in presentation drawings and computer-aided design (CAD) skills necessary for professional practice in the design sector. Utilizes Rhino, Revit, Adobe Illustrator, Adobe Photoshop, and Twinmotion.

title: Design: Creative Revolution
description: Introduction to comprehensive design: design formed by the cross pollination between artistic and design processes. Introduces examples of comprehensive design as they form within the entity of the city and as they take form within objects of design (e.g. clothing, furniture, architecture, automobiles). Also introduces students to methods and approaches in 2D and 3D design execution. Credit given for only one of AMID-C 181 or SOAD-C 181.

title: Introduction to Comprehensive Design
description: P: SOAD-A 103 and SOAD-C 180; or consent of instructor.  Focuses on skill building across the spectrum of modalities: drawing, physical model building, and 3D modeling. Uses human-centered design strategies to explore how human perceptions and activities affect design decisions. Credit given for only one of AMID-C 280 or SOAD-C 280.

title: Project Management for Comprehensive Design
description: P: SOAD-C 280.  Covers key components of the management and execution of a creative project. Focuses on scope management, project time management, and communications.

title: Contemporary Issues in Comprehensive Design
description: P: SOAD-C 280; or consent of instructor.  Focuses on the combination of empathy, creativity, and rationality needed to understand the context of a problem and develop solutions that fit the particular context. May be repeated with different topics for a maximum of 8 credit hours in AMID-C 380 and SOAD-C 380.

title: Topical Issues in Collaborative Design (1–4 cr.)
description: P: SOAD-C 380; or consent of instructor.  Employs design thinking strategies to understand challenges facing a community partner. The class will then focus on a co-design participatory approach, with project partners to offer solutions to the project. Classes may partner with regional organizations and companies. May be repeated with different topics for a maximum of 8 credit hours in AMID-C 381 and SOAD-C 381.

title: Special Problems in Comprehensive Design
description: P: SOAD-C 381; or consent of instructor.  Leverages a unique combination of design thinking and strategic planning to design, prototype and test solutions to real problems faced by communities, businesses, individuals, and organizations. Provides opportunity to act as a consultant for a real client to design and propose a strategic plan, implement accepted plans, and evaluate the impact of the solution. May be repeated with different topics for a maximum of 6 credit hours in AMID-C 480 and SOAD-C 480.

title: Intensive Seminar in Comprehensive Design
description: P: SOAD-C 380; or consent of instructor.  Provides opportunities to engage in the research, testing, and prototyping required for larger scale projects. Completed projects may range from public art to installations to furniture design. Includes discussions of materiality, fabrication, form, use, and structure as part of design and fabrication.

title: Interior Design Studio I
description: Focus on critical and analytical ability, reasoned understanding and rational application of design elements and principles, development of vocabulary, attitudes, and skills basic to design and interior design. Credit given for only one of SOAD-D 170 or SOAD-D 168.

title: Design Studies: Form and Function
description: In this introductory course, students will learn about the decisions designers make to shape the objects they encounter every day and the effects of those decisions on those of us who interact with them. Features lectures and hands-on projects examining designs as diverse as toothbrushes, iPods, bicycles, and buildings. Credit given for only one of AMID-D 191 or SOAD-D 191.

title: Sustainable Design
description: Provides a basic understanding of issues related to sustainability in product design and the built environment. In particular, it encourages critical thinking with regard to an understanding and application of the life cycle analysis, as well as introducing organizations dedicated to sustainability and the rating systems they use. Credit given for only one of AMID-D 192 or SOAD-D 192.

title: 3D Modeling for Design and Digital Fabrication
description: Principles of 3D modeling applied across subject-specific design boundaries. Studio methods for solving 3D geometric problems from technical drawings and models. Students create free-form curves, surfaces, and solids employed in interior design, fashion design, furniture design and digital fabrication using software such as Rhinoceros NURBS and digital fabrication equipment. Credit given for only one of AMID-D 201 or SOAD-A 201.

title: Interior Visualization
description: Drawing techniques focusing on creative/inventive sketching and rendering for interior design. Both freehand and digital techniques will be explored. Students will use the language of drawing to improve the process of conceptual design, design development, visualization, and communication. Credit given for only one of AMID-D 202 or SOAD-D 202.

title: Design Methods
description: P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-D 170.  Introduction to the basic techniques of design research and evaluation, emerging trends in design thinking, and state-of-the-art design methodologies. Credit given for only one of AMID-D 263 or SOAD-D 263.

title: Digital Architectural Drawing (3–4 cr.)
description: Basic 2D and 3D drawing and modeling skills for professional practice of interior architectural design using professional, industry standard software: document and database generation, computer-aided drafting, modeling and design. Credit given for only one of AMID-D 264 or SOAD-D 264.

title: Materials and Components of Interior Design
description: P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-D 170 and SOAD-D 264; or consent of department.  Course fee required.  Provides basic understanding of the appropriate use of materials specified for the interior environment. Particular emphasis placed on the vital role interior designers play in providing for the health, safety, and welfare of those inhabiting interior spaces.  Credit given for only one of SOAD-D 267 or SOAD-D 277.

title: Interior Design Studio II
description: P: A grade of C- or higher in SOAD-D 168 or SOAD-D 170; and SOAD-D 264; or consent of Area.  Introduction to design processes, technical requirements (e.g., stairs), space planning, and design graphics through residential design projects.

title: Interior Design Studio III
description: P: A grade of C- or higher in SOAD-D 271 and SOAD-D 264; or consent of Area.  Introduction to programming, technical requirements (e.g., code and accessibility guidelines), and evidence-based design through commercial design projects.

title: Special Topics in Interior Design (1–3 cr.)
description: Selected topics in an area of interior design. Topics will vary. May be repeated with different topics for a maximum of 9 credit hours in AMID-D 290 and SOAD-D 290.

title: Design, Culture, Context, and Diversity
description: P: A grade of C- or higher in SOAD-D 272; and in SOAD-D 321 or SOAD-D 322 or SOAD-D 323; or instructor consent.  In-depth exploration of the relationship between design, systems thinking, culture, social issues, and diversity.

title: Green Building Concepts and Technologies
description: Examines topics related to green building design and technologies, including sustainable sites, water efficiency, energy efficiency, materials and resources, indoor environmental quality, and LEED Exam preparation.

title: Critical Theory, Writing and Thinking for the Studio Practice
description: P: Completion of the English composition requirement.  Introduction to critical writings and writing on the subject of art and design and critical analysis of art and design. Credit given for only one of AMID-D 304 or SOAD-D 304.

title: Health and Wellness by Design
description: Focuses on knowledge and tools to integrate the health and well-being of people into the built environment.  Content includes design strategies, evidence-based approaches, case studies, and application of knowledge.

title: Design History I
description: Survey of design history from prehistoric times to the Gothic era with emphasis on architecture, interiors, furniture, material culture, decorative arts, and art.  Introduction to culturally appropriate frameworks for understanding design and designed objects. Credit given for only one of SOAD-D 321 or SOAD-D 261.

title: Design History II
description: Survey of design history from the Renaissance through the nineteenth century with emphasis on architecture, interiors, furniture, material culture, decorative arts, and art.  Introduction to culturally appropriate frameworks for understanding design and designed objects. Credit given for only one of SOAD-D 322 or SOAD-D 262.

title: Design History III
description: Survey of design history from the twentieth century to the present with emphasis on architecture, interiors, furniture, material culture, decorative arts, and art.  Introduction to culturally appropriate frameworks for understanding design and designed objects. Credit given for only one of SOAD-D 323 or SOAD-D 268.

title: Lighting Interior Spaces I (1.5 cr.)
description: P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-D 271, SOAD-D 272, AMID-D 271, or AMID-D 272; and MATH-D 116 and MATH-D 117, MATH-J 113, MATH-M 106, MATH-M 118,  MATH-M 119, MATH-M 211, MATH-S 118, MATH-S 211, MATH-V 118, or MATH-V 119..  Introduction to the general principles, factors, and techniques of interior lighting, including planning, analysis, calculation methods, and aesthetics in the residential context. Field trips required. Credit given for only one of AMID-D 335 or SOAD-D 335.

title: Lighting Interior Spaces II (1.5 cr.)
description: P: SOAD-D 335 or AMID-D 335 with a grade of C- or higher.  Introduction to the general principles, factors, and techniques of interior lighting, including planning, analysis, calculation methods, and aesthetics in the commercial design context. Field trips required. Credit given for only one of AMID-D 336 or SOAD-D 336.

title: Building Detailing and Systems
description: P: SOAD-D 272.  Course fee required.  Survey of sustainability and building systems related to thermal comfort, well-being, acoustics, electrical and communication systems, and water and waste systems.  Develops a basic understanding of systems, specifications, and details. Credit given for only one of SOAD-D 360 or SOAD-D 302.

title: Architectural, Interior, and Furniture Design Studies
description: P: SOAD-D 168 or AMID-D 168 with a grade of C- or higher; or consent of department.  A survey of the history of design at three scales: architecture, interiors, and furniture design. The underlying concepts behind historically prominent work, and the commonalities of design at different scales, will be reviewed. Throughout the course, students will cultivate their own visual memory to guide their design thinking and work. Credit given for only one of AMID-D 365 or SOAD-D 365.

title: Lighting Interior Spaces
description: P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-D 271, SOAD-D 272; and MATH-D 116 and MATH-D 117, MATH-J 113, MATH-M 106, MATH-M 118,  MATH-M 119, MATH-M 211, MATH-S 118, MATH-S 211, MATH-V 118, or MATH-V 119.  Course fee required.  Introduction to the general principles, factors, and techniques of interior lighting, including planning, analysis, calculation methods, and aesthetics in the commercial and residential contexts. Credit given for only one of SOAD-D 366 or SOAD-D 336.

title: Professional Practices and Documentation in Interior Design
description: P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-D 272.  Course fee required.  Survey of interior design careers, business procedures and practices and of professional values and standards. Development of professional communication through employment application materials and construction documentation. Practice in construction specification writing, drawing referencing, and the relationship of drawings in integrated construction documents. Credit given for only one of SOAD-D 369 or SOAD-D 469.

title: Interior Design Studio 4 (3–5 cr.)
description: P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-D 263 or AMID-D 263; and SOAD-D 272 or AMID-D 272; and SOAD-D 277 or AMID-D 277; and MATH-M 118, MATH-M 119, or equivalent.  Design of dwellings for individuals and groups in a variety of contexts. Integration of social, technical, spatial, and environmental factors. Course fee required. Credit given for only one of AMID-D 373 or SOAD-D 373.

title: Interior Design Vertical Studio
description: P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-D 264, SOAD-D 272, and (SOAD-D 267 or SOAD-D 277).  Course fee required.  Introduction to team-based design strategies in response to interdisciplinary, technical, spatial, environmental, and culturally framed design problems, with special emphasis on socio-economic conditions, diverse populations, and global contexts. May be repeated for credit for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: Interior Design Technical Studio
description: P: A grade of C- or higher in SOAD-D 272; and in SOAD-D 267 or SOAD D-277.  Course fee required.  Application of building codes, construction methods and materials, specification, lighting, and building systems to the design of interior space.  Course fees and field trips required.

title: Advanced Digital Architectural Drawing (3–4 cr.)
description: Course fee required.  Advanced CAD visualization for professional design practice: three-dimensional modeling and visualization, image processing, animation, and multimedia presentation.

title: Interior Design Capstone Studio
description: P: A grade of C- or higher in SOAD-D 374 and SOAD-D 375.  Course fee required.  Individual development of technically, culturally, and evidence-based design solutions to complex design problems.

title: Interior Design Studio 7 (3–5 cr.)
description: P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-D 469 or AMID-D 469; and SOAD-D 475 or AMID-D 475.  Course fee required.  Design of habitats and work spaces for special populations.  Credit given for only one of AMID-D 476 or SOAD-D 476.

title: Advanced Special Topics in Interior Design (1–3 cr.)
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Advanced selected topics in an area of interior design. Topics will vary. May be repeated with a different topic for a maximum of 6 credit hours in AMID-D 485, AMID-D 490, and SOAD-D 485.

title: Sustainable Practices in Art, Design, and Merchandising
description: Examines the issues of sustainability that artists, designers, product developers, retailers and consumers confront as they go about their work of creating and sustaining creative lives and commerce. Creates and utilizes cross-disciplinary discussion about sustainable practices in art, design, and merchandising.

title: Design for Visual Accessibility
description:
An introduction to inclusive design practices, focusing on visual accessibility through research, user-centered methods, user experience design, prototyping, and digital
fabrication.

title: Entrepreneurship in the Creative Industry
description: Explores the safeguarding of intellectual property rights (IPR) and translating creative practice into viable commercial offerings. Covering the core IPR regimes relevant to commercial art, including copyright, trademark, and design/utility patents, at a glance, alongside brand strategy, pricing, and channel selection.

title: Cross-Disciplinary Workshops in Art, Design, and Merchandising
description: Introduces and explores a theme or topic from two varying disciplinary or artistic perspectives within art, design, and merchandising. Course content is designed around variable topics such as social engagement, narrative, cultural context, branding, form/material, and body/identity. May be repeated with a different topic for a maximum of 12 credit hours of SOAD-A 211 and SOAD-E 211.

title: Introductory Textile Science: Laboratory
description: P: SOAD-A 100, SOAD-M 109, SOAD-M 209, or AMID-R 100 with a grade of C- or higher.  Analysis and application of concepts learned in lecture: fibers, fabrications, finishes, and properties. Special attention paid to aesthetics of textiles and materials for fashion design. Laboratory course. Course fee required. Credit given for only one of AMID-F 202 or SOAD-F 202.

title: Materials for Merchandising and Fashion Design
description: P: SOAD-A 100, SOAD-M 109, SOAD-M 209, or AMID-R 100 with a grade of C- or higher.  Lecture plus laboratory. Course fee required.  Textile fibers, yarns, fabrication, and finishes, textile processing and properties; selection and care of textiles for human use.  Credit given for only one of AMID-F 203 or SOAD-F 203.

title: Fashion Studio I
description: P or C: SOAD-F 203.  Introduction to pattern development, fitting, fabric manipulation, and construction of original fashion designs.

title: Fashion Design I: Research and Illustration
description: P: SOAD-A 100; and SOAD-F 203; and Fashion Design Major or Minor.  Studio class. Course fee required.  Exploration of design inspiration and precedent research methods. Use of various media to communicate fashion design through technical drawing and illustration.

title: Special Topics in Fashion Design (1–3 cr.)
description: P: Consent of department.  Selected topics in an area of fashion design. Topics will vary. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours in AMID-F 290 and SOAD-F 290.

title: Fashion Studies: Topics in Cultural Analysis
description: Dress as a reflection of the physical, social, psychological, and aesthetic environment of various non-US cultures; responses to and uses of dress as an aspect of global culture and individual behavior. Topics vary. May be repeated with a different topic for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: Fashion Studio II
description: P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-F 203, SOAD-F 207, and SOAD-F 213; and Fashion Design Major or permission of instructor.  Studio class. Course fee required.  Intermediate pattern development, fitting, fabric manipulation, and construction of original fashion designs.  May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: Fashion Studio III
description: P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-F 328 and SOAD-F 303; and (MATH-D 116 and MATH-D 117) or MATH-J 113 or MATH-M 106 or MATH-M 118 or MATH-M 119 or MATH-M 211 or MATH-S 118 or MATH-S 211 or MATH-V 118 or MATH-V 119..  Studio class. Course fee required.  Advanced pattern development, fitting, fabric manipulation, and construction of original fashion designs.  May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours in AMID-F 305 and SOAD-F 305.

title: Experimental Fashion Studio: Tailoring
description:
P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-F 328 and SOAD-F 303
.  Studio class. Course fee required.  Techniques in contemporary and traditional tailoring. Credit given for only one of AMID-F 306 or SOAD-F 306.

title: History of Dress
description: P: SOAD-F 203 or AMID-F 203.  Dress in Western civilization relating to social, economic, political, and religious events from early civilization through seventeenth century.  Credit given for only one of AMID-F 311 or SOAD-F 311.

title: Fashion Studies: Autobiographies of Dress and the Body
description: P: at least junior standing or consent of instructor.  Critical reading of autobiographies from diverse perspectives (race, class, gender, religion, disability, etc.) in order to develop empathy for the values and needs of diverse fashion consumers. Includes reflection/discussion about the practice of reading and writing autobiographies.

title: Fashion Design Research
description:
P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-F 303 and SOAD-F 328
.  Studio class. Course fee required.  Application of design research (historical, cultural, aesthetic, technological, market-driven, etc.) in the process of developing new fashion designs, drawing on both creative and critical thinking. Prior knowledge of materials, clothing construction methods, and tools for visual communication is essential.

title: Experimental Fashion Studio: Shape and Foundation
description: P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-F 328 and SOAD-F 303 .  Course fee required.  Fundamental principles of design for inner-shaping and foundation; manipulation of a variety of materials and techniques to produce original designs featuring complex super-structures. Studio class.  Credit given for only one of AMID-F 325 or SOAD-F 325.

title: Experimental Fashion Studio: Accessories
description: P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-F 213; or consent of instructor.  Course fee required.  Fundamental principles and techniques for design of accessories including gloves, hats, handbags, jewelry, and masks; manipulation of a variety of materials to produce original accessory design. Studio class.  Credit given for only one of AMID-F 326 or SOAD-F 326.

title: Experimental Fashion Studio: Digital Applications
description: P: SOAD-F 213 or AMID-F 213.  Course fee required.  CAD applications used to create digital fashion fabrics, fashion lines, and fashion portfolios for the apparel and textile industries. Studio class.  Credit given for only one of AMID-F 328 or SOAD-F 328.

title: History of Fashion
description: P: SOAD-F 203 or AMID-F 203.  Dress in Western civilization relating to social, economic, political, and religious events in the eighteenth through twenty-first centuries; emphasis on the emergence and evolution of haute couture. Credit given for only one of AMID-F 340 or SOAD-F 340.

title: Fashion Studies: Aesthetic Analysis
description: P: At least junior standing; or consent of instructor.  Aesthetics of dress as a medium of visual communication. Considerations include aesthetic norms, color psychology, elements and principles of design, appearance management strategies, and design process.

title: Fashion Studies: Fashion and Social Issues
description: P: At least junior standing.  Explores aspects of contemporary fashion and how societal issues manifest in fashion phenomena of the 20th and 21st centuries. Areas to be explored may include diversity and inclusivity, the effects of media on body image, the impact of new technologies on fashion and lifestyle trends, future fashion, and gender fluidity.

title: Collection Management for Historic Textiles and Costumes
description: P: SOAD-F 203 or AMID-F 203.  Curatorial methods and procedures in museum collection management specifically related to historic textiles and costumes. Topics may vary to include accessioning, cataloging, artifact research and identification, exhibition, conservation, computer utilization. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours in AMID-F 402 and SOAD-F 402.

title: Fashion Studies: Fashion Theory
description: P: At least junior standing; or consent of instructor.  Survey of methodologies and research techniques for analysis of fashion diffusion and change. Credit given for only one of AMID-F 406 or SOAD-F 406.

title: Experimental Fashion Studio: Draping
description: P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-F 328 and SOAD-F 303.  Course fee required.  Fashion design through the use of fundamental principles and processes of draping; manipulation of a variety of materials to produce original draped designs. Studio class.  Credit given for only one of AMID-F 408 or SOAD-F 408.

title: Fashion Studies: Behavioral Analysis
description: P: At least junior standing; or consent of instructor.  Basic concepts and theories from social psychology are used to study how clothing affects the self and others.

title: Fashion Design III: Presentation and Analysis
description: P: SOAD-F 305 or AMID-F 305 with a grade of C- or higher.  Course fee required.  Planning and presenting a fashion collection, development of accompanying research, and preparation of professional portfolio materials to build professional relationships and develop a compelling personal narrative to aid students in the job search. Studio class.   Credit given for only one of AMID-F 417 or SOAD-F 417.

title: Advanced Special Topics in Fashion Design (1–3 cr.)
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Advanced selected topics in an area of fashion design. Topics will vary. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours in AMID-F 485, AMID-F 490, and SOAD-F 485.

title: Introduction to Retail Design and Merchandising
description: Introduction to the retail cycle related to the interdisciplinary study of and research in apparel and textiles. Examines social and economic theories underlying the fashion business, retail merchandising principles, consumer behavior, and contemporary retail issues. Credit given for only one of AMID-R 100 or SOAD-M 100.

title: Apparel Industries
description: Introduction to the retail cycle related to the interdisciplinary study of and research in apparel and textiles. Examines social and economic theories underlying the fashion business, retail merchandising principles, consumer behavior, and contemporary retail issues. Credit given for only one of AMID-R 209, SOAD-M 109, or SOAD-M 209.

title: Manufacturing and Quality Analysis
description: P: SOAD-M 109, SOAD-M 209, AMID-R 100, or AMID-R 209 with a grade of C- or higher.  Focuses on the manufacturing and product development stage of the retail cycle. This stage comprises apparel merchandising history, technology, textiles, performance, value, and quality analysis. Credit given for only one of AMID-R 204 or SOAD-M 204.

title: Professional Development in Merchandising
description: P: Class standing of Sophomore or higher; and SOAD-M 209.  Professional techniques and competencies applicable to careers in the retail industry are explored while students participate in exercises to develop presentation skills and marketing themselves to, and in, a retail organization. Credit given for only one of SOAD-M 219, SOAD-M 319 or AMID-R 319.

title: Digital Merchandising
description:
P: C- or higher in SOAD-M 109
.  Exploration and analysis of both customer behavior and business strategies in digital merchandising. Application of fundamental digital principles to the development of innovative digital merchandising strategies.

title: Brand Management and Advertising
description: P: At least junior standing.  Strategic marketing communication centered on building and maintaining brand equity, encompassing all stages of the retail cycle. Credit given for only one of AMID-R 308 or SOAD-M 308.

title: Strategies in Retail Promotion
description: P: SOAD-M 109, SOAD-M 209, AMID-R 100, or AMID-R 209; and at least sophomore standing.  Application of design elements and principles to commercial display and development of creative visual merchandising techniques; various media explored. Credit given for only one of AMID-R 309 or SOAD-M 309.

title: Principles of Retail Merchandising
description: P: Grade of C- or higher in SOAD-M 109, SOAD-M 209, AMID-R 100, or AMID-R 209; and in BUS-K 201; and in MATH-M 118, MATH-M 119, or equivalent; and at least sophomore standing.  An examination of the contemporary retail environment as it relates to principles, such as the profit and loss analysis, retail math, markups, markdowns, and turnover. Comprehensive analysis and strategy development using retail merchandising principles are emphasized. Credit given for only one of AMID-R 315 or SOAD-M 315.

title: Retail Product Management
description: P: SOAD-M 109, SOAD-M 209, AMID-R 100, or AMID-R 209; and at least sophomore standing.  Design thinking will be used to study strategic product management in the current retail environment. Function and practices in product distribution, merchandising and retail selling to the end use consumer segments of the retail cycle will be examined as they relate to contemporary retail store management and operations. Credit given for only one of AMID-R 316 or SOAD-M 316.

title: Product Development
description: P: SOAD-M 204 or AMID-R 204; and at least junior standing; or consent of department.  Functions and practices of product development; understanding and defining the customer; researching fashion trends from color and fabric to silhouette; identifying how and when to apply trends to targeted customers; practical application of executing concepts into finished products. Credit given for only one of AMID-R 327 or SOAD-M 327.

title: Digital Product Development (1–3 cr.)
