Activities are designed for elementary school and special populations. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 200 or THTR-D 200.

title: Dance Practices III
description: P: THTR-D 112.  Designed to allow the student to develop a higher level of technical proficiency, with an emphasis on the application and analysis of various movement principles as they relate to dance and performance.

title: Dance Practices IV
description: P: THTR-D 211.  An extension of principles examined in THTR-D 211 with an emphasis on style and performance, through the use of longer and more complex movement sequences. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 212 or THTR-D 212.

title: Urban Street Dance
description: Exploration of The Old School style of House Street Urban Hip Hop dance and movement. Class will be non-stop paced movement that is very loose and bouncy. Many of the combinations will be repetitive with variations of themes. Water bottle and sweat towel required. May be repeated for a maximum of 8 credit hours.

title: Approaches to Dance-Making I
description: P: THTR-D 161.  An introduction to the craft of choreography. Students explore basic rhythmic, spatial, and dynamic materials through group improvisation and individual studies. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 221 or THTR-D 221.

title: Approaches to Dance-Making II
description: P: THTR-D 221.  Elements for forming and structuring movement into complete dances. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 222 or THTR-D 222.

title: Introduction to Dance Studies
description: Introduction to Dance Studies establishes literacy in dance as an art-form. Using a global perspective, students will explore the elements of dance and how they manifest in culture from social settings to the concert stage. The roles of dancer, choreographer and spectator will be studied in multiple contexts of the discipline of theatrical, social and cultural dancing. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 231 or THTR-D 231.

title: Dance in Human Society
description: The investigation of dance in other cultures will expand our understanding of dance as an integral component of human expression. Through seminars, lectures, and embodied research, students will gain an understanding of dance as art, religious practice, social customs, and political action.

title: Intermediate Tap Dance
description: P: THTR-D 154; or consent of instructor.  Second-level course in the technique and styles of tap dance and the use of tap dance steps to create original choreography. Development of tap dance as a musical and visual art form. Credit given for only one of HPER-E 254 or THTR-D 254.

title: Intermediate Modern Dance
description: P: THTR-D 155; or consent of instructor.  Intermediate modern dance technique, stressing knowledge and application of movement principles essential to dance training. May be repeated for a total maximum of 3 credit hours in HPER-E 255 or THTR-D 255.

title: Intermediate Jazz Dance
description: P: THTR-D 156; or consent of instructor.  A continuation of instruction in the modern jazz style of movement. Special emphasis on movement efficiency, precision, and clarity. Credit given for only one of HPER-E 256 or THTR-D 256.

title: Contemporary Dance Workshop
description: P: Consent of instructor.  A wide variety of movement experiences in technique, movement analysis, movement correctives, and social dance forms. May be repeated for a maximum total of 4 credit hours in HPER-D 201, THTR-D 201, and THTR-D 301.

title: A Somatic Approach to Contemporary Ballet Practices
description: Focuses on anatomically based ballet technique, with respect for individual physical differences. Emphasizes classical ballet vocabulary, along with sound alignment, deep muscular core support, and efficient movement mechanics. Explores contemporary trends in ballet performance and choreography. May be repeated for a maximum total of 21 credit hours in HPER-D 100, THTR-D 100, and THTR-D 302.

title: Dance Practices V
description: P: THTR-D 212.  Designed as an intensive immersion in both theory and practice of dance technique. Through readings, discussions, writings, and experiential learning, students gain an intimate understanding of both the artistic and physiological principles that guide performance in modern dance. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 311 or THTR-D 311.

title: Dance Practices VI
description: P: THTR-D 311.  Continuing intensive immersion course in both the theory and practice of dance techniques. Through readings, discussion, writings, and experiential learning, students gain an intimate understanding of both the artistic and physiological principles that guide performance in modern dance. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 312 or THTR-D 312.

title: The Hoofers Ensemble
description: P: Permission of instructor.  Combines basic tap technique and traditional tap history with new, innovative styles being created in the tap world today. Teaches knowledge of tap education at a basic level along with basic musical structure, traditional and vintage tap choruses, and an overview of American tap history. May be repeated for a maximum of 12 credit hours

title: Twentieth-Century Concert Dance
description: Survey of Twentieth-Century concert dance history. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 332 or THTR-D 332.

title: Cultural Choreographies
description: P: THTR-D 109.  A foundation in contemporary global and urban styles of dance that are interwoven and fused within professional contemporary dance such as hip-hop, krump, Israeli ga ga style, and others. Prepares pre-professional dancers to be well versed physically and intellectually in current contemporary, global dance styles. May be repeated for a maximum of 12 credit hours in THTR-D 241 or THTR-D 341.

title: Dance Pedagogy
description: P: THTR-D 211.  Study of various approaches, methods, and materials for teaching concert dance on the secondary level, including procedures for evaluation. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 351 or THTR-D 351.

title: Musical Resources for Dance
description: Presentation of criteria for selection of music for dance accompaniment. Aural evaluation of various musical styles, with emphasis on their adaptability as dance accompaniment. Visual-aural analysis of musically accompanied choreography. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 353 or THTR-D 353.

title: Advanced Tap Dance
description: P: THTR-D 254; or consent of instructor.  For students who have taken intermediate tap and want to continue learning the art of rhythm tap. Others with a solid foundation in tap are also welcome. Explores more complex rhythmic patterns and skills through routines, challenges, and improvisation. Students will be required to present routines of their own at the end of the semester. May be repeated for a maximum of 2 credit hours for any combination of HPER-E 354 and THTR-D 354.

title: Advanced Modern Dance
description: P: THTR-D 255; or consent of instructor.  Advanced techniques in modern dance with emphasis on performance of movement patterns and individual creative work. May be repeated for a maximum of 4 credit hours in HPER-E 355 or THTR-D 355.

title: Indiana University Contemporary Dance Theatre
description: A modern dance repertory company. May be repeated for a maximum total of 4 credit hours in HPER-D 261, THTR-D 261, and THTR-D 361.

title: Dance Practices VII
description: P: THTR-D 312.  Instructor-guided practicum in current issues regarding contemporary dance technique and trends in performance. Provides a practical culminating experience for senior dance majors as they prepare to enter the professional world. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 411 or THTR-D 411.

title: Dance Practices VIII
description: P: THTR-D 411.  Instructor guided practicum in current issues regarding contemporary dance technique and trends in dance performance. Provides a culminating experience for senior dance majors as they enter the professional world. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 412 or THTR-D 412.

title: Choreographic Performance Project
description: P: THTR-D 222.  Under faculty guidance, each student is responsible for initiating and developing a completed work for concert performance. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 421 or THTR-D 421.

title: Dance Production I
description: Basic orientation to technical theatre, specifically for dance. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 441 or THTR-D 441.

title: Music and Soundscape Creation for Dance
description: Teaches choreographers the skills needed to create, edit, and play music and soundscapes for dance concerts, as well as the basics of computer-based audio software and sound system design.

title: Advanced Jazz Dance
description: P: THTR-D 256; or consent of instructor.  A continuation of instruction in the modern jazz style of movement with special emphasis on movement precision, clarity, and performance style. Students should be able to demonstrate an understanding of alignment and a mastery of advanced jazz-level combinations, demonstrate advanced and improved movement memory, demonstrate advanced and improved clarity, precision, and musicality, and show stylistic  differences through the performance of movement combinations May be repeated for a maximum of 4 credits in HPER-E 456 or THTR-D 456.

title: Jazz Dance Repertory
description: P: THTR-D 256; or consent of instructor.  Provides students an opportunity to learn original choreography from dance faculty members, and collaborate in the creation of original choreography in a classroom environment. Students experience the choreographic process firsthand, as well as build on technical, performance, and learning skills. Credit given for only one of HPER-E 457 or THTR-D 457.

title: Methods of Movement Analysis
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Methods of analyzing movement in dance. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 461 or THTR-D 461.

title: Senior Dance Summary
description: P: Open to senior dance majors only.  Explores current issues and trends in dance performance and education through discussion and practicum. May be repeated up to 2 times for a maximum of 2 credit hours.

title: Advanced Choreographic Investigations
description: P: THTR-D 222.  Provides the dedicated and advanced choreography student with an opportunity to create their own choreography, utilizing the principles of dance composition and incorporating personal insights and ideas into the works. The resultant product will be informed by contemporary and cutting-edge practices within the field. Each student works individually with a faculty mentor.

title: Topics in Dance (1–4 cr.)
description: Studies in special topics not ordinarily covered in other departmental dance courses. May be repeated for credit up to eight times with different topics.

title: Professional Body Work Certification
description: Provides students the opportunity to gain knowledge in the arena of somatic body work as well as pursue professional certifications in specific areas. Certifications in yoga, as granted through the Yoga Alliance, and  Kane School of Pilates will be offered. May be repeated for a maximum of 12 credit hours.

title: Introduction to Theatre
description: Exploration of theatre as collaborative art. Investigation of the dynamics and creativity of theatre production through plays, theatrical space, and cultural context, with particular attention to the roles and interaction of the audience, playwrights, directors, actors, designers, producers, and critics.

title: Script Analysis for the Theatre
description: Close analysis and study of both traditional and nontraditional play texts in terms of structure, genre, style, character, themes, language, dramatic action, and dramatic event. Plays are examined from the point of view of the actor, director, designer, producer, critic, scholar, and audience.

title: Great Performances in Film
description: In-depth examination of a single actor's performances in films spanning the course of his/her career. Includes methods of character creation as well as the history and theory of acting and performance.

title: Introduction to Musical Theater
description: P: THTR-T 100.  Introduction to and an analysis of musical theatre as a collaborative art form, as a profession, and as an academic discipline, highlighting its various forms and its function during the course of its development from Show Boat to The Band's Visit.

title: Beginning Musical Theatre Dance
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Focuses on the foundations of dance, beginning with strength building exercises, stylized movement across the floor, and dance combinations that will further  the understanding of the myriad of styles within the Musical Theatre Dance canon. May be repeated for a maximum of 4 credit hours.

title: Acting I: Fundamentals of Acting
description: Introduction to theories and methodology through sensory awareness, physical and vocal exercises, improvisations, and scene study. Credit given for only one of THTR-T 120 or THTR-T 121.

title: Acting I for Majors: Introduction to Acting
description: P: Major in theatre and drama or departmental consent.  An accelerated-level course. Supplementary theories and methodology to expand the introduction to sensory awareness, physical and vocal exercises, improvisation, and scene study. Credit given for only one of THTR-T 120 or THTR-T 121.

title: Performance as Art and Design
description: Introduces the process of design as it relates to the performing arts and entertainment. Explores methods of collaboration, design thinking, concepts of style, and perceptions of both individual and socio-historical aesthetic as it relates to storytelling for an audience.

title: Stage Make-up Design
description: Introduction to basic theories and skills in stage makeup. Survey covers corrective, period, basic prosthetics, and other effects used in performance.

title: Music and Sound Design in Horror and Science Fiction
description: Examines the history, creation, and implementation of sound design and scoring for science fiction and horror, which have developed a rich language and tradition over the past 100 years. Teaches the basics of how to create sound effects for film and television.

title: Pilates for the Performing Artist
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Based on the practice of Pilates, with a focus on increasing core strength and stabilization, muscle balance, tone, coordination, flexibility and injury prevention techniques. Technique will be explored in basic dance movement the performing artist practices, to connect the body work somatics. May be repeated for a maximum of 8 credit hours.

title: Musical Theatre Dance Technique
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Ballet barre students will gain placement, proper alignment, port de bras, strength, extension, articulation and rhythm as well as developing the basis for musical theater dance styles. Center floor will concentrate on ballet technique as it relates to musical theater dance styles: Can-Can, Tarantella, Waltz, Theatrical Tango.		 May be repeated for a maximum of 8 credit hours.

title: IU Broadway Cabaret (0–1 cr.)
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Touring performance ensemble dedicated to the American Songbook and musical theatre literature. May be repeated for a maximum of 4 credit hours.

title: Theatre Dance
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Develops dance techniques required for a musical theater career. Warm-up techniques of Katherine Dunham and Jack Cole combined with classical ballet technique. Exercises to develop stamina, line, isolation, extension, turns, jumps, and hinges at and away from the barre. Focus on discovering ability to embody emotion and character in movement.		 May be repeated for a maximum of 12 credit hours in THTR-T 204 and THTR-T 307.

title: Swing Nation: Swing & Understudy Education for the Musical Theatre Stage
description:
Study and development of a swing and understudy process for the musical theatre stage. Teaches the ability to sing a variety of vocal lines and execute the different choreography tracks within a single show. Explores the intersection of developing your own character combined with fulfilling specific blocking and directorial needs.
 May be repeated for a maximum of 4 credit hours.

title: Acting II: Scene Study
description: P: A grade of C or higher in THTR-T 120 or THTR-T 121; or audition.  Techniques for expressing physical, intellectual, and emotional objectives. Study, creation, and performance from varied dramas.

title: Stage Management I
description: P: THTR-T 100, THTR-T 101, or THTR-T 125.  Explores the role and function of the stage manager in theatrical production. Provides the basic skills to begin work in the field of stage management. Emphasizes organization, documentation, and dissemination of information.

title: Introduction to Playwriting
description: P: THTR-T 101 or consent of instructor.  Introduction to the creative process and the fundamentals of playwriting, including structure, character creation, dialogue, conflict, dramatic action, and setting. Requires in-class exercises and the writing of several short plays. Credit given for only one of THTR-T 254 or THTR-T 453.

title: Applied Theatre
description: Explores how aspects of drama, theatre, and performance practice and theory may be used within multiple aspects of society from the physical and mental health industry to business, science, and education.

title: Production Seminar I
description: P: Consent of department.  A required course for students involved with any mainstage production or reading sponsored by the Department of Theatre and Dance.  Roles vary per production but may include designing, technology, managing, construction, dramaturg, choreography, assisting, etc. If registered for credit on the production through another course, this course is not required. May be completed a maximum of 4 times. S/F grading.

title: Design and Technology Projects in Theatre and Drama (1–3 cr.)
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Creation and development of individual student-related projects in design and technology culminating in a portfolio presentation. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: Musical Theatre Workshop
description: P: Audition and consent of instructor; THTR-T 101; THTR-T 120 or THTR-T 121; and THTR-T 220.  Focus on synthesizing acting, singing, and dancing into one performance technique. Emphasis will vary according to needs of students. May be repeated for a maximum of 12 credit hours.

title: Musical Theatre Dance Styles I
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Dance styles of the 1920s through the 1950s are explored and performed, including the works of choreographers Fred Astaire, Hermes Pan, Agnes DeMille, Jerome Robbins, and Gower Champion. May be repeated for a maximum of 12 credit hours.

title: Musical Theatre Dance Styles II
description:
P: Consent of instructor.  A continuation of THTR-T 301, Dance styles of the 1950s through present day are explored and performed, including the works of choreographers Bob Fosse, Michael Bennett, Bob Avian and Susan Stroman.
 May be repeated for a maximum of 12 credit hours.

title: Advanced Musical Theatre Dance Styles
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Provides fast-paced instruction in advanced-level musical theater dance. Explores a wide range of musical theater dance styles, advanced jazz, and repertoire from the rich canon of classic musicals as well as new interpretations of classic musical theater choreography are taught. May be repeated for a maximum of 24 credit hours.

title: Voice for Musical Theatre
description: P: B.F.A. major or consent of instructor.  Private study of vocal performance focusing on the development of the vocal instrument for musical theatre repertoire. May be repeated for a maximum of 8 credit hours.

title: Musical Theatre Workshop for non-BFA Students
description: Explores the basics of vocal and performance techniques in musical theatre. Guides non-music majors in developing their voice, performance skills, and self-expression, aiming to prepare at least one audition-ready song by semester's end through practice, feedback, and live performance analysis.

title: Choreography for the American Musical
description: Examines the history and development of American musical theater dance before and during "The Golden Age" of the American musical (1943-1964). Analyzes the craft and methodology of musical theater dance creation, including examples from current Broadway seasons.

title: Musical Theatre Studio (1–3 cr.)
description: Topics course focusing on the creation of musical theatre productions. Topics may include form, function, and analysis; directing musicals; choreographing musicals; and writing for the musical theatre. May be repeated with a different topic for a maximum of 12 credit hours.

title: Introduction to Movement for the Theatre
description: P: THTR-T 120 or THTR-T 121.  Drawing from multiple movement modalities, this course provides a foundation of physical training designed to make students aware of the world around them, and of their bodies in space. The training will increase student flexibility, strength and stamina through exercises and techniques designed to prepare artists for fully embodied performance.

title: History of American Musical Theatre
description: Traces the development of American musical theatre and explores the repertoire in its cultural, economic, theatrical, social, religious, ethnic, political, and theoretical context.

title: Creating Choreography for Musical Theatre
description: P: Dance experience; and consent of instructor.  Cultivates choreographic skills for the musical theatre stage. Analyzes the craft and methodology of musical theater dance through exploring composition and how dance functions in storytelling. Provides practice in the interpretation of choreographic language with the goal of constructing dance pieces to build a choreographic repertoire.

title: Acting III: Advanced Scene Study
description:
P: THTR-T 220 with a grade of C or higher; or audition
.  Emphasizes ensemble acting, study and performance of early modern Realism, and contemporary American texts.
 Credit given for only one of THTR-T 319 or THTR-T 420.

title: Costume and Character in London Theatre
description: P: Consent of the department.  Overseas theatre studies in London. Experience theatrical character development through costume design. Survey social influences on costume and dress worn by characters through history including contemporary trends and dress. Field trips to such locations as Bath and Stratford.

title: Performance in Digital Media
description: P: THTR-T 220.  Provides experience in alternative media projects that are as necessary to actors as stage and film, such as voiceover, audiobook narration, and animation. Covers skills to optimize social media content, as well as personal websites, and how to produce original content both at home and in a studio environment.

title: Voice and Speech
description: P: THTR-T 120 or THTR-T 121.  R: Sophomore standing.  Introduction to voice production. Emphasizes relaxation, breathing, and the production of vocal sounds; deals with vocal habits and cultural holds through exercises and vocal workouts. Introduction to phonetics and practical work with text while freeing the voice and redeveloping a passion for language.

title: Introduction to Scenic Design
description: P: THTR-T 101 and THTR-T 125; or consent of instructor.  An entry-level studio course introducing the process of scene design, concept development, and the communication and presentation of theatrical ideas.

title: Stage Management II
description: P: THTR-T 229.  An examination of the organizational and logistical aspects of productions of musical events from the perspective of production and stage management. Examining case studies in musical theatre, ballet, modern dance, opera, and concerts, students gain insight into management strategies and develop critical thinking skills.

title: Costume Construction I
description: P: THTR-T 125.  Introductory to intermediate costume construction techniques, theatrical wardrobe crew basics, and information on fabric and materials used in theatrical costumes. Credit given for only one of THTR-T 230 or THTR-T 334.

title: Stage Lighting Design
description: P: Sophomore standing; THTR-T 101 and THTR-T 125; or consent of instructor.  Lecture and laboratory.  Introduction to the process of determining and implementing a lighting design. Analytical skills, concept development, design methods, lighting technology, and practical applications are covered.

title: Fundamentals of Costume Design
description: P: THTR-T 125 or consent of instructor.  Course fee required.  Intensive study of costume design in mainstream theatre. Projects in collaborative aesthetics in design and practical application, rendering techniques, and visual communication. Credit given for only one of THTR-T 337 or THTR-T 433.

title: Directing I: Fundamentals of Directing
description: P: THTR-T 101; and THTR-T 120 or THTR-T 121.  R: THTR-T 125, THTR-T 230, and THTR-T 335.  Introduction to theories, process, and skills (text analysis, working with actors, staging, and telling a story), culminating in a final project.

title: Introduction to Sound Design for the Theatre
description: P:  THTR-T 125; or consent of instructor.  Within the framework of theatrical environments, this course introduces the student to acoustic principles, audio equipment used in theatrical productions, psycho-acoustical considerations, understanding sound signals and how to manipulate them, computer sound mixing systems, and sound design principles.

title: Entertainment Technology and Production
description: P: THTR-T 125.  Modern technologies are used to enhance or make possible the entertainment experience. The theatrical design process and engineering coalesce in the development, design, and implementation of solutions for live production including theatre, dance, opera, ballet, musicals, exhibits and performance art.

title: Musical Theatre Studio I: Acting the Song
description: P: Admission to the Musical Theatre BFA; and THTR-T 121.  Focuses on the objective of the character singing the song by identifying the needs, obstacles, tactics, expectations, and given circumstances of the song. Particular attention is paid to deep analysis of the text of the song before it is performed.

title: Musical Theatre Studio II: Scene Study
description: P: THTR-T 351.  Focuses on the relationship between characters in the Musical Theatre scene with particular focus on the transition from spoken text to sung text, how it happens, and why. Scenes from existing musicals will be analyzed, scanned, and performed.

title: Musical Theatre Studio III: Audition Technique
description: P: THTR-T 352.  Focuses on audition technique, including building of an audition repertoire, creating and maintaining a clear and concise résumé, establishing a system of analysis of audition and callback materials, mock-callbacks with guest casting and music directors. Integrates business practices and artistic choices unique to the MT performer.

title: Musical Theatre Studio IV: Advanced Methods
description: P: THTR-T 353.  Further honing of skills in musical theatre scene and song, breaking down and clarifying genres and styles according to composer, lyricist, librettist, and time period of the musical. Particular emphasis is placed on style and genre.

title: Theatre Production Studio (1–3 cr.)
description: P: THTR-T 101; one of THTR-T 125, THTR-T 229, THTR-T 230, or THTR-T 335; and consent of instructor.  Intermediate, hands-on production course that teaches the theatre production process within a specific area focus. Students gain proficiency within a specific theatre production area and are engaged in a managerial role in an academic season production, including the development of production documentation. Students gain skills in communication, organization, and coordination. May be repeated in a specific area once (at a higher managerial level), and in multiple areas for a total of 3 credit hours.

title: History of Theatre and Drama I
description:
P: THTR-T 100, THTR-T 101, or consent of instructor
.  Worldwide development of theatre and drama from beginnings to present. Study of theatre arts and culture institutions; focus on historical context for plays and performances; emphasis on research methods in theatre history. Beginnings to circa 1700.

title: History of Theatre and Drama II
description: P: THTR-T 100 or consent of instructor.  Development of theatre and drama in Western world from beginnings to present. Study of theatre arts and cultural institutions; focus on historical context for plays and performances; emphasis on research methods in theatre history.  Circa 1700 to present.

title: Foundations for Teaching Theatre and Drama
description: P: Consent of theater education specialist. C: EDUC-M 303.  The accumulation of a range of theatre production and teaching experiences through the building of a portfolio of experiences and resource materials.

title: Reading, Research, Performance for Honors (1–12 cr.)
description: P: Approval of departmental honors advisor.  May be repeated for a maximum of 12 credit hours.

title: Musical Theatre Senior Showcase
description: P: Audition and consent of instructor.  Introduction to business and marketing techniques for success in professional theatre. Specific instruction in the audition process culminating in performance for agents and casting directors.

title: Senior Musical Theatre Dance Audition (2–3 cr.)
description: P: Admission to the BFA in Musical Theatre; and instructor consent.  Focuses on the art of the dance audition in the professional world of musical theatre. Maintains and improves dance skills through daily jazz/ballet warmups and quick study dance combinations in various styles. Includes research on current Broadway choreographers, ongoing practice in professional audition techniques, and live musicians. May be repeated once.

title: Stage Combat
description: P: THTR-T 311 or THTR-T 410.  Complete basic training in the safety techniques of theatrical and film violence, based upon the accepted practices and principles founded by the Society of American Fight Directors and other internationally recognized stage combat organizations. Emphasis placed on acting the fight, storytelling and historical armed and unarmed combat.

title: Introduction to Flat Patterning
description: P: THTR-T 430 or consent of instructor.  Introduces and develops costume patternmaking skills. Teaches the process for drafting basic slopers (bodice, sleeve, skirt, collars) using individual measurements. Explores the manipulation techniques to alter slopers to achieve individual design elements.

title: Introduction to Draping
description: P: THTR-T 430 or consent of instructor.  Develops basic to intermediate costume draping skills and fabric manipulation to achieve the most desirable results. Promotes effective communication between the costume technician and costume designer. Examines costume sketches, research and photos to enhance the ability to interpret and create accurate and acceptable costumes.

title: Movement for the Theatre I
description: P: THTR-T 120 or THTR-T 121.  Introduction to fundamental principles and methodologies of movement training for the actor. Emphasis on connecting to imagery and impulse, conditioning, centering and alignment, physical characterization, and kinesthetic awareness in relation to the self, other actors, and the space.

title: Movement for the Theatre II
description: P: THTR-T 410.  A continuation of advanced training for physical acting with more detailed instruction in the works of Michael Chekhov, Rudolf Laban, and Jerzy Grotowski. Training in martial arts, stage combat, dramatic acrobatics and yoga helps students build a bridge between training and performance (i.e., research and laboratory).

title: Stage Combat II
description: P: THTR-T 404.  An advanced exploration of theatrical and human, individual and group movement through the lens of heightened dramatic action. Employs a variety of approaches to recreate violent situations from dramatic literature. Additional certification opportunities with the Society of American Fight Directors may be available.

title: Rendering I
description: P: Consent of instructor.  An in-depth study into the rendering and communication of design choices for costuming. Special focus on communication of character, spirit and detail of a figure and its costume, and on drawing animated and evocative figures that clearly demonstrate a mastery of character development and expression. Strong introduction into the medium of watercolor.

title: Acting V: Audition & Rehearsal Techniques
description: P: THTR-T 319 or THTR-T 419 plus Instructor approval.  Covers techniques for actors to locate, secure, and prepare auditions. Includes rehearsal preparation and strategies and specifically covers intimacy for the stage. Professional guest artists and administrators will be invited to contribute to the course.

title: Acting IV: Acting Shakespeare
description:
P: THTR-T 319 and THTR-T 325 and THTR-T 410; or audition.
.  Emphasis on skills for analyzing and performing heightened language, verse and prose text by Shakespeare. Continued development of the actor's skillset and versatility.
 Credit given for only one of THTR-T 320 or THTR-T 419.

title: Acting V: Acting Style I
description: P: THTR-T 320 or THTR-T 419.  Techniques for performing comedy with an emphasis on verbal and other physical skills; introduction to period and style of plays from Aristophanes to Goldoni.

title: Acting VI: Acting Style II
description: P: THTR-T 319 or THTR-T 419 and instructor permission.  Focuses on techniques for performing in a variety of theatrical styles. Styles may include but are not limited to: comedy styles, theatrical non-realism, TYA, and devised theatre.

title: Introduction to Theatrical Drafting
description: P: THTR-T 125 and THTR-T 326; or written consent of instructor.  A studio course consisting of both traditional hand drafting techniques and digital CAD techniques as they are used in theatrical production communication.

title: Fundamentals of Scenic Design
description: P: THTR-T 326 or consent of instructor.  Work in line, color, and composition using historical conventions as the basis for contemporary scenic statements. Emphasis on period style and presentational forms.

title: Production Management
description: P: THTR-T 229 or consent of instructor.  Examination of the role of production manager in theatrical productions. Emphasis on budgetary and schedule planning and management. Application of human resource management theory also a significant component of the course.

title: Costume Technology II
description: P: THTR-T 125 and THTR-T 334; or consent of instructor.  Further development of costume construction techniques for interested students who have satisfactorily completed THTR-T 230. Provides a foundation of sewing, craft, fitting, and patternmaking techniques for use in developing a construction project and performing production assignments.

title: Studies in Stage Management
description: P: Consent of instructor.  An examination of the stage management requirement and regulations for non-standard production styles. Each year the topic varies and may include spectacle, theme park, festival, or other large scale entertainment. On site observation and experience is a key portion and requirement of the course. May be repeated with different topics for a maximum of 9 credit hours.

title: Costume Design II
description: P: Consent of instructor.  No laboratory/technology component.  Intensive study of costume design in mainstream theatre. Projects in collaborative aesthetics in design and practical application rendering techniques and visual communication.

title: Historic Costumes for the Stage
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Survey of historical costume in western civilization, ancient Mesopotamian cultures through the twentieth century. Taught from sociohistorical perspective and applied to performance theory.

title: Electronics for Theatre
description: P: Consent of instructor.  Fundamentals of electricity and electronics as applied to theatre. Investigation of current technology for theatrical performance, including power distribution, control systems, and creative applications for lighting, sound, special effects, and mechanized scenery.

title: Scene Painting I
description: P: Consent of instructor.  A studio course introducing techniques and equipment of theatrical scenic painting.

title: Advanced Stage Lighting Design
description: P: THTR-T 335.  Stage lighting design-concept development, presentation, and implementation are emphasized, along with advanced lighting techniques and approaches. A practicum will be assigned.

title: Acting for the Camera
description: P: THTR-T 220; and THTR-T 325.  Audition required. An exploration of the fundamentals of acting for the camera, designed to develop students' on-camera acting skills.ï»¿

title: Directing II: Advanced Directing
description: P: THTR-T 340 or consent of instructor.  Theory and practice from play selection to performance. Emphasis on rehearsal and performance of varied dramatic material.

title: Directing III: Directing Style
description: P: THTR-T 442.  Emphasis on analysis, interpretation, rehearsal, and presentation of plays from a range of styles and periods.

title: Historic Costume II
description: P: THTR-T 434.  Second half of the costume history curriculum. A survey of Western costume from late seventeenth-century Europe through current day. Focuses on historical costume as it relates to the society and period in which it lived and its relevance to designing for the performing arts.

title: Voice and Dialects
description: P: THTR-T 325.  Investigation of dialects as a distinctive form of pronunciation, language, structure, and vocabulary identified with a geographical area or social class as it applies to the requirements of theatrical clarity and dramatic interpretation. Working with varied texts using points of resonance, vocal focus, and rhythm to unlock the actors' expressive tools. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours.

title: Aesthetics of Sound Design
description: P: THTR-T 347 or consent of instructor.  Focuses on theatrical design concept development and implementation. Students are exposed to various design challenges and processes for sound design in a range of production styles and approaches.

title: Voice in Performance
description: P: THTR-T 325 and consent of instructor.  A voice class for the performing artist employing the techniques of artists such as Patsy Rodenburg, Cicely Berry, and Kirsten Linklater. Exploration of the life and muscularity of varied texts. Opening the voice to match the music of what one is saying while learning to identify and respect the writer's intentions through voice and language.

title: Stage Rigging I
description: P: THTR-T 125 or consent of instructor.  Survey of structural engineering terminology and methods as applicable to common rigging practice in the theatre and entertainment industry.

title: Stage Rigging II
description: P: THTR-T 451.  Hands-on course designed to familiarize students with the operation and maintenance of typical rigging equipment. Class work includes counterweight system operations, pin-rail operations, rope and knot basics, wire-rope basics, chain hoist basics, and arena-type rigging basics.

title: Playwriting Workshop
description: P: THTR-T 254 or consent of instructor.  Prior playwriting experience helpful, but not required.  Consideration of dramaturgical antecedents and practical and theoretical problems. Creation of a full-length play.

title: Screenwriting
description: Structural analyses of cinematic models, culminating in the creation of an original full-length narrative screenplay.

title: Development of Dramatic Art I
description: Dramatic art in Western world from beginnings to the present. Study of dramatic genres, plays in production, and theatrical approaches to plays of the past. I: Classical to early Renaissance drama; II: Late Renaissance to modern drama; III: Modern and contemporary drama.

title: Development of Dramatic Art II
description: Dramatic art in the Western world from beginnings to the present. Study of dramatic genres, plays in productions, and theatrical approaches to plays of the past.Â  II: Late Renaissance to Modern Drama.

title: Development of Dramatic Art III
description: Dramatic art in Western world from beginnings to present. Emphasis on drama in theatre of its origin and on methods of interpreting plays of the past in contemporary theatre. III. 1940 to present.

title: Topics in Theatre History and Literature
description: Studies in special topics related to theatre history, theory, and literature not ordinarily covered in other departmental courses. May be repeated with different topics for up to 6 credit hours.

title: ProTools for Theatre and Music
description: P: One course from MUS-A 100, THTR-T 347, or THTR-T 447.  An introduction to the recording software ProTools for use in theatre sound design and music production.

title: Understructures for Historical Garments
description: P: THTR-T 430.  Study of various understructures of dress from 1500-1900, including materials and techniques of construction. Final project requires the creation of an ensemble of understructures from a specific piece of research.

title: Period Patternmaking and Construction
description: P: THTR-T 430.  Study of various patternmaking techniques and shapes of women's garments from 1500-1920. Final project requires the creation of an ensemble based on specific research.

title: Asian Performance
description: An in-depth study of the major forms and styles of traditional and contemporary theatre, dance, and puppet performances from around Asia (broadly defined).

title: Globalization, Theatre, and Performance in Southeast Asia
description: Explores theories and effects of globalization in Southeast Asia through an examination of traditional and contemporary theatre and performance.

title: Methods and Materials for Teaching High School Theatre and Drama
description: P: THTR-T 378.  Methods, techniques, content, and materials applicable to the teaching of theatre and drama in secondary, middle, and junior high schools.

title: Introduction to Stage Properties
description: Covers the basics of running a properties shop. Students will learn script analysis for props; making lists; communication with stage management, designers, and technical personnel; budget breakdowns; and using local resources for the build/buy/borrow/pull process of properties production.

title: Topics in Theatre and Drama (1–3 cr.)
description: Studies in special topics not ordinarily covered in other departmental courses. May be repeated for credit up to four times with different topics.

title: Production Seminar II
description: P: Consent of department.  A required course for students involved with any mainstage production or reading sponsored by the Department of Theatre and Dance. Roles vary per production but may include designing, technology, managing, construction, dramaturg, choreography, assisting, etc. If registered for credit on the production through another course, this course is not required. May be completed a maximum of 6 times. S/F grading.

title: Working in the Profession
description: P: THTR-T 319.  Preparation for a professional theatre career. Topics include auditions, managers, unions, finances, regional theatre, professional courtesy, and realities of the work environment.

title: Reading, Research, Performance for Honors  (1–12 cr.)
description: P: Approval of departmental honors advisor.

title: Creative Work in Summer Theatre (1–3 cr.)
description: P: Consent of department.  Work in summer theatre productions.  May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours in THTR-T 390 and THTR-X 373. S/F grading.

title: Teaching Internship in Theatre
description: heatre (1 cr.) P: Junior or senior standing; an A- or higher in THTR-T 100; and consent of department.  A UTIN is an undergraduate who assists faculty in the Department's teaching mission, guiding peers and modeling academic success. UTINs develop leadership skills, pedagogical insight, and a working relationship with a faculty mentor. Students interested in graduate/professional school or teaching will explore attributes necessary in related careers, earning transferable skills. May be repeated for a maximum of 3 enrollments and 3 credit hours. S/F grading.

title: Practicum in Community Dance Partnerships
description: P: THTR-D 200.  Dance majors will design, implement and teach a dance education program for community partners in local social service settings that promote well-being and dance appreciation through personal, and physical creative expression. Credit given for only one of HPER-D 421, THTR-D 497, or THTR-X 476.

title: Independent Study in Theatre and Drama (1–6 cr.)
description: P: 12 credit hours in theatre and drama, departmental grade point average of 3.000 or above, and consent of instructor.  Readings, performances, experiments, and reports in area of student's special interest. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 credit hours in THTR-T 490 and THTR-X 490.
